name: Ensure used KMSKeys are not exposed through publicly accessible VMs
description: Datastore encryption keys reachable through roles attached to publicly accessible virtual machines.
body:
### Security objective

This control ensures that **datastore encryption keys are not reachable through identities attached to publicly manageable VMs**.

### Exposure path

The assessment correlates three signals: a VM reachable from the internet on management ports, the VM identity or role having KMS permissions, and the key being used by a datastore. This is a compound-risk control, because the key may not be public by itself, but the public VM creates a practical route to it.

### Why this deserves priority

If a publicly reachable VM is compromised, its runtime identity can become the attacker's identity. When that identity can decrypt or administer datastore keys, the incident can move from host compromise to data compromise.

A secure design breaks at least one part of the chain: private management access, no broad KMS permissions on VM roles, and key usage scoped to the datastore service identity or a dedicated workload identity.
remediationDescription:
## Remediation

Break the path from an internet-exposed VM to datastore encryption keys. The check verifies for public VM exposure plus a role, identity, or permission path to a KMS key used by a datastore.

### AWS

1. Open the affected EC2 instance and identify its security groups and IAM instance profile role.
2. Remove public SSH/RDP ingress from the security group. Use private access, AWS Systems Manager Session Manager, VPN, or a bastion instead.
3. Review the instance profile role policies and remove unnecessary KMS permissions such as kms:Decrypt, kms:Encrypt, kms:GenerateDataKey, kms:CreateGrant, kms:*, or broad resource access.
4. Scope KMS permissions to the datastore service role or a dedicated workload role, not the public VM role.
5. Validate the security group and role policy changes:

~~~bash
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids <security-group-id> --region {{asset.region}}
aws iam list-attached-role-policies --role-name <instance-role-name>
aws iam list-role-policies --role-name <instance-role-name>
~~~

References:
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/control-access.html
- https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager.html

The control is satisfied when the public VM no longer has a permission path to a datastore KMS key, or when the VM is no longer publicly reachable.

### GCP

1. Identify the Compute Engine instance, attached service account, firewall rules, and reachable Cloud KMS keys.
2. Remove public ingress firewall rules for SSH/RDP or restrict them to trusted private ranges.
3. Remove cloudkms.cryptoKeyDecrypter, cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter, cloudkms.admin, or broad project roles from the VM service account when not required.
4. Grant KMS permissions only to the datastore service account or workload identity that needs the key.
5. Validate IAM and firewall state:

~~~bash
gcloud compute firewall-rules list --filter="sourceRanges:0.0.0.0/0 OR sourceRanges:::/0"
gcloud projects get-iam-policy <project-id> --flatten="bindings[].members" --filter="bindings.members:<service-account-email>"
~~~

References:
- https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/iam
- https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewalls

The control is satisfied when the exposed VM service account no longer has access to datastore KMS keys or the VM is no longer publicly exposed.

### Azure

1. Identify the VM, its public IP exposure, NSG inbound rules, managed identity, and Key Vault access path.
2. Remove public SSH/RDP NSG rules or restrict them to trusted private networks through VPN, Bastion, or Just-In-Time VM access.
3. Remove Key Vault permissions such as get, unwrapKey, wrapKey, decrypt, encrypt, or broad RBAC roles from the VM identity unless required.
4. Grant key access to the datastore identity, managed identity, or service principal that actually owns the encryption workflow.
5. Validate network and Key Vault assignments:

~~~bash
az network nsg rule list --nsg-name <nsg-name> --resource-group <resource-group>
az role assignment list --assignee <principal-id> --scope <key-vault-or-key-scope>
~~~

References:
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/rbac-guide
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/windows/connect-logon#use-azure-bastion

The control is satisfied when an internet-reachable VM identity no longer has a path to datastore keys.

### Alibaba

1. Identify the ECS instance, security group, RAM role, and KMS key permissions used by the datastore.
2. Remove public SSH/RDP ingress from the ECS security group or restrict access to trusted private CIDRs.
3. Remove broad KMS permissions from the ECS RAM role or attached policies.
4. Grant KMS permissions only to the datastore service role or least-privilege workload role.
5. Validate security group rules and RAM policies:

~~~bash
aliyun ecs DescribeSecurityGroupAttribute --RegionId <region-id> --SecurityGroupId <security-group-id>
aliyun ram ListPoliciesForRole --RoleName <role-name>
~~~

References:
- https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/en/ecs/user-guide/delete-a-security-group-rule
- https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/en/kms/key-management-service/developer-reference/api-kms-2016-01-20-overview

The control is satisfied when the public ECS instance no longer has a permission path to the datastore KMS key, or when the instance is no longer public.
